_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code. Note: TMS 3.2 Q2 'Was the clinical notes and serum calcium report indicative of the procedure?' is marked as expected: false for hyponatremia/hypernatremia due to serum calcium report not being a mandatory document for these conditions as per the document's table, indicating it would not be 'indicative' for these specific procedures.
clinical_indications: Serum sodium levels greater than 145 mmol/L
Symptoms of hypernatremia (tachypnea, sleeping difficulty, feeling restless)
Unreplaced fluid loss through the skin and gastrointestinal tract

preauth.label: Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment

claim.label: Detailed Indoor case papers with treatment details

ppd_preauth.q: Was the Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment submitted?
Was the serum calcium report submitted?
Was the serum electrolyte report submitted?
Was the patient's blood report suggestive of electrolyte imbalance?
Was the clinical notes and serum calcium report indicative of the procedure?

cpd_claim.q: Was Detailed Indoor Case Papers with Treatment details submitted?
Was the post treatment serum calcium report submitted?
Was the post treatment serum electrolytes report submitted?
Was the Detailed Discharge Summary submitted with the date of the follow-up mentioned?

common_queries: Clinical notes regarding evaluation findings, indications, or planned line of treatment are missing or incomplete.
Pre-treatment other serum electrolytes report is missing or does not indicate hypernatremia (e.g., serum sodium <= 145 mmol/L).
Detailed indoor case papers with treatment details are missing or incomplete.
Post-treatment other serum electrolytes report is missing or does not show improvement.
Detailed discharge summary is missing or does not include the date of follow-up.
Patient's blood report is not suggestive of electrolyte imbalance (specifically hypernatremia).

additional_information.min_doctor_qualification_desirable: DNB / MD (General Medicine / Pediatric Medicine/Endocrinology and Metabolism)
clinical_key_pointers: Electrolytes are essential for basic life functioning such as maintaining electrical neutrality in the cells, generation, and conduction of action potentials in the nerves and muscles.
Sodium, potassium, and chloride are the significant electrolytes along with magnesium, calcium, phosphate, and bicarbonates. Electrolytes come from our food and fluids.
These electrolytes can have an imbalance, leading to either high or low levels, disrupting normal bodily functions and potentially leading to life-threatening complications.
Hypernatremia is diagnosed when serum sodium levels are greater than 145 mmol/L and can present with symptoms like tachypnea, sleeping difficulty, and feeling restless.
Rapid sodium corrections can have serious consequences like cerebral edema and osmotic demyelination syndrome.
additional_information.referral_criteria: Referral of cases to the appropriate level of care as required for treatment under PMJAY.
